title: SEGMENT 14 — When you want a small molecule instead: synthetic dyes

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — When you want a small molecule instead: synthetic dyes
So fluorescent proteins are wonderful.
But they're not the only fluorescent labels in the toolbox.

For many experiments — especially in fixed-cell immunofluorescence, and increasingly in live imaging through clever schemes I'll describe in a moment — the right tool is not a fluorescent protein at all but a small synthetic dye.
So let me give you a quick tour of those.
Synthetic fluorescent dyes go back over a century.
The modern ones come from a few major chemical families, each with characteristic properties.
The fluoresceins and rhodamines are classical dyes built on a chemical scaffold called xanthene.
Fluorescein, discovered in 1871, is one of the oldest synthetic fluorescent dyes still in heavy use.
It's bright, water-soluble, and emits green around 520 nanometers.
Rhodamines emit red.
These dyes were the backbone of fluorescence microscopy for most of the twentieth century and remain widely used.
The cyanine dyes — Cy3, Cy5, Cy7 — are a different family whose color can be tuned across the spectrum by adjusting the number of carbons in a central chemical chain.
They're bright, narrow in their emission, and they're the workhorses of single-molecule fluorescence and DNA sequencing chemistries.
Cy5 has the helpful property of blinking under specific buffer conditions, which made it one of the original STORM dyes.

The Alexa Fluor series, from Molecular Probes (now part of Thermo Fisher), is a designed family of dyes optimized across the spectrum — from Alexa Fluor 350 in the ultraviolet to Alexa Fluor 790 in the near-infrared.
They were engineered to be brighter, more photostable, more pH-resistant, and more spectrally pure than the old classics.
For most modern fixed-cell immunofluorescence work, Alexa Fluor dyes attached to secondary antibodies are the default.
The Atto dyes, from a German company, are another high-performance family with excellent brightness and photostability.
And finally, the Janelia Fluor dyes, or JF dyes — JF549, JF646, and others — are the current state of the art.
They were designed by Luke Lavis's lab at Janelia.

They're bright, photostable, cell-permeable, and they incorporate some clever chemistry — using a modification called azetidine substitution — that both improves brightness and lets the dye switch between non-fluorescent and fluorescent forms depending on whether it's free in solution or bound to its target.
That switching gives extremely low background.
So when do you want a synthetic dye instead of a fluorescent protein?
You want a dye when brightness and photostability are paramount.
For single-molecule tracking, for super-resolution, for deep-tissue work, the best dyes outperform the best fluorescent proteins by significant margins on both axes.
You want a dye when you're doing fixed-cell immunofluorescence.
Fluorescent proteins don't help you label endogenous untagged proteins.
Antibodies do, and antibodies carry dyes.
You want a dye when you need a fluorophore in a system where genetic engineering is impractical — clinical samples, environmental samples, some animal models.

You want a fluorescent protein when you need genetic encoding — when the label must propagate through cell divisions, when you want to express the tag only in specific cell types defined by a promoter, when no exogenous reagent can reach your sample, or when you want to encode an entire sensor like GCaMP whose function depends on the protein architecture itself.
The two approaches are complementary, not competing.

A typical modern experiment might use GCaMP — a genetically encoded calcium indicator — to image activity in a specific neuron population, while also using a synthetic dye to label some structural feature for context.
The protein gives you cell-type specificity.
The dye gives you brightness.
Each tool does what it does best.
